name: Hank Thompson
technical_background: Industrial engineering background with Lean/Six Sigma Black Belt certification. Strong process improvement and ERP skills. Comfortable with data analysis in Excel and basic VBA. Familiar with IoT sensor data and SCADA systems from factory operations. No AI, ML, or modern programming experience.

current_profile.summary: VP of Operations with 18 years of experience in manufacturing and supply chain management. Industrial engineering degree with Lean/Six Sigma Black Belt certification. Led major transformation programs including ERP rollouts (SAP), lean manufacturing initiatives, and warehouse automation projects. Manages a 200+ person operations organization across 3 manufacturing plants. Familiar with IoT sensor data and SCADA systems from factory floor operations but does not work with the data technically. Comfortable with Excel for operational analysis and basic VBA for automating reports. Understands predictive maintenance concepts from vendor presentations but has no hands-on ML knowledge. Does not use any AI tools currently — interested but has not started.
technical_skills: ERP systems (SAP: production planning, inventory, MRP)
Process improvement and value stream mapping
Excel (advanced: operational analysis, Pareto charts, control charts)
IoT/SCADA system familiarity (read dashboards, not configure)
Supply chain management and logistics optimization
Capital expenditure planning and ROI analysis
current_profile.ai_experience: Minimal AI experience. Has attended vendor presentations on predictive maintenance and computer vision for quality inspection but has not used any AI tools. Understands the concept of using data for prediction from Six Sigma statistical background but has no ML knowledge. Has heard of ChatGPT from industry peers but has not tried it. Starting from near-zero AI exposure.

learning_intent: Wants to lead AI transformation in operations: predictive maintenance, demand forecasting, quality inspection automation, and supply chain optimization. Goal: become AI-literate enough to scope high-ROI AI projects, partner effectively with data science and engineering teams, define KPIs and governance for AI deployments, scale successful pilots, and manage workforce impacts of automation. Not seeking to become a data scientist — seeking to be the operations executive who drives AI adoption strategically and responsibly.

target_jd.title: AI Strategy Leader (Operations / Smart Factory)
requirements: Identify high-ROI AI use cases in operations and prioritize implementation roadmap
Ensure data readiness and integration across OT/IT systems for AI deployment
Define KPIs, success metrics, and governance frameworks for AI deployments in operations
Scale successful AI pilots from single plant to enterprise-wide implementation
Communicate benefits, risks, and workforce impacts of AI adoption to leadership and workers
Partner with data science and engineering teams to scope and deliver AI solutions
Evaluate AI vendor solutions for manufacturing (predictive maintenance, vision QC, demand forecasting)
Manage change and workforce transition as AI automates operational tasks
Ensure safety and compliance of AI systems in manufacturing environments
Build data-driven culture and AI literacy across the operations organization

expected_skill_gaps.description: Understand AI vs traditional software, ML concepts, model limitations, and how AI differs from deterministic automation and Six Sigma statistical methods.
High-level understanding of data pipelines, cloud primitives, and API integration — enough to have informed conversations with technical teams.
LLMOps concepts at leadership level: monitoring, incident response, deployment gates, and versioning for production AI systems.
Facilitate AI workshops with operations teams, write AI project proposals, and lead cross-functional alignment on AI roadmaps.
AI use-case selection and prioritization for operations, ROI measurement frameworks, and AI enablement strategy for the organization.
Understanding AI evaluation at a leadership level: why it matters, basic evaluation types, and monitoring for deployed AI systems.
AI governance fundamentals and risk framing for manufacturing AI, including safety considerations for autonomous systems.
Building AI literacy and staying current with AI developments relevant to manufacturing and operations.
